coredata.dc:title: A Cauchy perturbation cuckoo search particle filtering algorithm for remaining useful life prediction of lithium-ion battery considering capacity regeneration

Liu, Yun

coredata.dc:description: The remaining useful life prediction of the lithium-ion battery can evaluate the battery's reliability, identify the occurrence of faults, and reduce battery risks. In this study, the particle filtering algorithm is employed along with the introduction of the Cauchy perturbation factor to enhance the local optimization capability of the cuckoo search algorithm. The improved cuckoo search optimization algorithm is utilized to transfer particles from prior distribution areas to the maximum likelihood area, resulting in the development of the Cauchy perturbation cuckoo search particle filtering algorithm. To achieve high-precision prediction of the remaining useful life, an attenuation model is established, taking into account the complex capacity regeneration phenomenon that occurs after a long period of resting of the lithium-ion battery storage. This model includes three types of capacity: underlying, recovery, and surface capacity. The experimental results, based on an aging dataset from the University of Maryland, demonstrate that the suggested algorithm provides clear advantages over widely used particle filtering and unscented particle filtering algorithms in terms of estimation accuracy. Additionally, it exhibits a fast convergence rate and low resampling rate, further enhancing its benefits.

row.entry: Test condition: Charging, discharging, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests.

Charging: Charging at 0.55 A constant current (CC) mode until battery voltage reaches 4.2 V, then continue charging at constant voltage (CV) mode until the charging current drops to 20 mA.
Discharging: Discharging at a constant current (CC) of 1.1 A until the voltages drop to 2.7 V.
Test environment: A1 and A2 were tested at 0.5 ℃, and A3 ∼ A6 were tested at 1 ℃. The humidity is maintained at 0 %.
Termination conditions: When the battery reaches the end of life (EOL) standard-30 % reduction of the rated capacity (1.1 Ah to 0.77 Ah).
Data purpose: The prediction of RUL.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Based on the above six datasets, the battery aging model considering capacity attenuation was further developed. The RUL prediction can be realized.

Comparison of prediction performance of different models in RUL.

head.ce:title: A Cauchy perturbation cuckoo search particle filtering algorithm for remaining useful life prediction of lithium-ion battery considering capacity regeneration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The remaining useful life prediction of the lithium-ion battery can evaluate the battery's reliability, identify the occurrence of faults, and reduce battery risks. In this study, the particle filtering algorithm is employed along with the introduction of the Cauchy perturbation factor to enhance the local optimization capability of the cuckoo search algorithm. The improved cuckoo search optimization algorithm is utilized to transfer particles from prior distribution areas to the maximum likelihood area, resulting in the development of the Cauchy perturbation cuckoo search particle filtering algorithm. To achieve high-precision prediction of the remaining useful life, an attenuation model is established, taking into account the complex capacity regeneration phenomenon that occurs after a long period of resting of the lithium-ion battery storage. This model includes three types of capacity: underlying, recovery, and surface capacity. The experimental results, based on an aging dataset from the University of Maryland, demonstrate that the suggested algorithm provides clear advantages over widely used particle filtering and unscented particle filtering algorithms in terms of estimation accuracy. Additionally, it exhibits a fast convergence rate and low resampling rate, further enhancing its benefits.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Cauchy perturbation cuckoo search particle filtering algorithm

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIB) have become extensively used energy sources or storage equipment in various fields [1], including electric vehicles, military equipment, aircraft, power stations, and space applications [2]. However, during the recycling process, factors such as electrode material corrosion, diaphragm aging, high temperatures, improper usage, or adverse external environments [3] can lead to internal micro-activity and result in short circuits. These issues ultimately degrade battery performance and compromise battery life and safety [4]. To address these concerns and make informed maintenance decisions, it is essential to research battery RUL prediction technology [5].

As the cycles proceed, repeated side reactions occur inside the battery, resulting in the consumption of electrolytes and deposition of side reaction products on the surface of active materials [6]. This phenomenon hinders the transport of electrons and ions, disrupting the chemical balance within the battery [7]. Consequently, the battery experiences an imbalance and a decline in capacity, which cannot be fully restored to its original state. These effects ultimately shorten the battery's service life [8].

The term "Remaining Useful Life" (RUL) refers to the number of cycles required for the performance condition of a lithium-ion battery to deteriorate and reach a specific failure threshold under a specific charge and discharge system [9]. The existing prediction technology for battery RUL can be classified into three groups: battery physical failure model-based, data-driven, and fusion technology-based prediction methods [10].

The RUL prediction method based on the physical failure model utilizes battery material properties, load conditions, failure mechanisms, and other information to establish a degraded model of battery performance, enabling RUL prediction [11]. This method can be further divided into two directions: the degradation mechanism model and the empirical degradation model, which describe battery aging behavior from different perspectives [12].

The prediction method based on the degradation mechanism model involves analyzing the electrochemical transformation of battery performance during operation [13]. It takes into account various aging factors affecting internal and external state variables, including voltage, current, state of charge, and electrolyte concentration, and establishes a battery aging model for RUL prediction [14]. For example, Lui, YH et al. [15] proposed a physics-based RUL prediction method for lithium-ion batteries, considering various degradation mechanisms such as the loss of active materials in the anode and cathode, as well as lithium-ion depletion. While this method is the most common approach within the physical failure model category and offers relatively straightforward calculations, its accuracy is relatively low since it cannot incorporate all influencing factors into mathematical formulas [16].

The prediction method based on the empirical deterioration model aims to establish a model that accurately reflects the trends in battery performance state parameters over time, including battery capacity, internal resistance, discharge end-off voltage, and more. This model utilizes a recursive relationship between the system's state variables at two moments, before and after, to predict the RUL of the battery [17]. Hong, GX et al. [18] proposed an iterative model of the generalized Cauchy (GC) process with long-term dependence (LRD), which measures LRD in the degradation process by utilizing fractal dimension and Hurst index to accurately describe the degradation trend of battery capacity. Compared to the mechanism model, the empirical degradation model is easier to obtain, reducing workload and having a wider application range [19]. However, this model is susceptible to noise and environmental disturbances, which can make it laborious to track the dynamic characteristics of the load accurately. Consequently, it may result in lower dynamic accuracy, robustness, and adaptability [20]. Building a precise model to describe battery capacity change is difficult due to various factors such as load [21], temperature [22], and capacity regeneration, and it requires a large amount of capacity degradation data for accurate modeling [23].

There have been some studies analyzing the impact of the capacity regeneration phenomenon. Fei Xia et al. [24] proposed that the voltage reconstruction results based on the second-order RC model are applied to incremental capacity analysis (ICA) and differential voltage analysis(DVA) analysis, effectively avoiding the influence of noise. To mitigate the impact of the capacity regeneration phenomenon, the State of Health (SOH) prediction results are decomposed using the Complete Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition with Adaptive Noise (CEEMDAN) method, and the decomposed residual is used as the input to improve the prediction accuracy of RUL. However, they have not discussed the different forms of capacity recovery phenomena. Addressing the shortcomings of existing research, this paper provides further analysis of the capacity recovery phenomenon and establishes models for underlying capacity, recovery capacity, and surface capacity.

The data-driven method for predicting the RUL of batteries is an important strategy. It involves analyzing and mining battery failure data and performance degradation data to estimate the RUL [25]. Battery capacity is widely utilized as one of the most common degradation factors in this approach. The data-driven methods based on failure data can be divided into two primary categories: the degradation data-based method and the multi-source data fusion method [26].

The prediction method based on degradation data focuses on battery attenuation or truncated failure time data [27]. It employs statistical analysis to determine the probability distribution of the remaining useful life (RUL) of the battery and then combines it with the battery's operating time to estimate the RUL [28]. Wang, FJ et al. [29] proposed a transferable RUL prediction method for LIB. This method utilizes a coding network to extract comprehensive battery degradation trend information from degradation data and employs cyclic consistency learning to align points with the same degradation degree across different batteries. This method facilitates obtaining the performance index for assessing the level of battery deterioration and has shown rapid development. However, achieving accurate prediction results requires a substantial amount of historical failure data or fault records based on batteries with high similarity. Without such data, the accuracy of the prediction results cannot be guaranteed [30].

The multi-source data fusion method involves various techniques, such as fusing historical and online data, fusing invalid and degraded data, and fusing prediction results from different models [31]. For instance, Jia et al. [32] proposed an RUL prediction method that combines sample entropy and relevance vector machine (RVM). This technique selects multiple variables, such as voltage sample entropy and current sample entropy, as inputs and uses the wavelet denoising method to preprocess the data. The entropy input in the RVM model is linearly weighted to build a prediction model that uses denoising entropy input to estimate the RUL. This approach enhances the effectiveness of data information and improves prediction accuracy. However, this method exhibits increased modeling complexity and parameter estimation challenges, which make it difficult to obtain an analytical expression for RUL prediction. Additionally, the algorithm's real-time robustness is relatively poor [33]. Moreover, this method may not accurately capture the degradation trend of battery performance, and its performance prediction may be less effective under more complex dynamic load conditions [34].

RUL prediction methods based on fusion technology have made significant advancements in recent years and have emerged as a popular area of research for predicting battery life [35]. These methods utilize hybrid prediction or integrate multiple methods to leverage the advantages of different approaches, resulting in improved performance [36]. Li, X et al. [37] proposed a dual filter-based RUL prediction method that combines a data-driven approach with a model fusion algorithm. The two fusion algorithm filters are used to iteratively update the degradation state of battery capacity and the model parameters of the least square support vector machine (LSSVM) concurrently in an online fashion. Such methods can be categorized as model-driven and data-driven fusion methods or as combinations of two or more complementary data-driven methods. The development and application of prediction techniques based on fusion technology have greatly enhanced the accuracy of RUL prediction and hold significant engineering value [38]. However, challenges remain, including complex model fusion parameters, extensive computational requirements, and uncertainties associated with fusion processes [39].

The accuracy of the projection of LIB's RUL is impacted by several factors. Firstly, there is a trade-off between model accuracy and complexity, and a comprehensive balance needs to be considered in modeling [40]. Secondly, the SOH of the battery cannot be measured directly and requires the introduction of other variables, leading to issues of variable selection and evaluation accuracy [41]. Thirdly, measurement error, noise, disturbance, and other factors in the experimental process can cause prediction errors and diverge the algorithm [42]. Finally, inconsistencies between batteries may lead to poor algorithm applicability [43].

At present, the field of battery life prediction is facing challenges and requires further research mainly in the following areas. Firstly, determining the availability judgment standard for monitoring data according to battery RUL prediction requirements, selecting the best performance degradation index for the battery, and achieving high-speed, real-time, and accurate online RUL prediction poses significant challenges [44]. Secondly, developing online life prediction methods for batteries under operating conditions involves appropriate degradation state identification methods, construction of performance degradation parameters, and battery dynamic condition modeling technology [45]. Thirdly, battery performance is greatly affected by low-temperature environments; however, there is a lack of research on battery life prediction in such conditions [46]. In this study, the capacity, which is widely utilized and reliable, is selected as the battery attenuation index. Considering the capacity regeneration phenomenon, we establish the underlying capacity, recovery capacity, and surface capacity models respectively. Additionally, the Cauchy perturbation factor is introduced into the cuckoo search algorithm to enhance local optimization performance. The improved cuckoo search algorithm is then integrated into the particle filtering algorithm to improve overall optimization performance and enhance algorithm accuracy. Finally, we achieve online RUL prediction of the battery and analyze the complex degradation process.

The structure of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 discusses the modeling of the underlying capacity, recovery capacity, and surface capacity of the battery. Section 3 introduces the improvement of the cuckoo algorithm and the establishment of the Cauchy perturbation cuckoo search particle filtering algorithm. Section 4 presents the RUL prediction of the battery in three low-temperature ranges. Finally, Section 5 summarizes the conclusions and outlines future research directions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attenuation modeling considering capacity regeneration phenomena

ce:section.ce:para: The battery of the aging dataset from the Center for Advanced Life Cycle Engineering (CALCE) of the University of Maryland is a kind of columnar lithium-ion cobalt acid battery [47], with a rated capacity of 1100mAh and a rated voltage of 4.2 V. The platform consists of a battery test system (NEWARE BTS-4000), a temperature controller (TT-5166th-7), a master computer, and a test battery. A series of charge and discharge tests were conducted on the battery at different low-temperature ranges until the battery reached complete degradation. The experimental setup is illustrated in Fig. 1.
In this paper, six datasets including CS2_33 ∼ CS2_38 renamed as A1 ∼A6 were selected. The experimental conditions and process are shown in Table 1 [48].

Yu Guo et al. [49] proposed a SOH estimation method based on double bi-directional long short-term memory (DBiLSTM) model. The paper focuses on the aging process of batteries and divides the battery capacity into two components: the smooth component and the fluctuation component. The smooth component exhibits a relatively stable decay process, while the fluctuation component is influenced by the resting time of the experiment and shows capacity regeneration phenomenon. The smooth component corresponds to the underlying capacity, while the fluctuation component corresponds to the recovery capacity and surface capacity. Based on the concepts of the smooth component and the fluctuation component proposed in this study, an analysis of the rate of change in the capacity of the CS2_33 battery is conducted for each cycle.

The CS2_33 battery's capacity change rate for each cycle (i.e., the ratio of capacity change to the charge-discharge time of that cycle) was analyzed to obtain Fig. 2 From the figure, it is evident that the battery's aging behavior may exhibit three distinct models:

Model 1 represents the normal degradation of the battery, characterized by a stable capacity change rate of −0.003 Ah/h. This model constitutes the majority of the battery's experimental cycles, leading to a relatively consistent degradation rate.
Model 2 corresponds to the recovery of battery capacity, manifested by a positive capacity change rate.

Model 3 depicts the post-recovery degradation of capacity, where the capacity change rate exceeds −0.003 Ah/h, significantly higher than the normal degradation rate. This model occurs after a capacity recovery event, and the rate gradually declines to the normal degradation rate over time.

The difference in degradation rates between Model 1 and Model 3 results in distinct behaviors of the recovery capacity compared to the original capacity of the battery. The recovery capacity is more prone to degradation. From a mechanistic perspective, the recovery effect on battery capacity is attributed to the redistribution of internal charges in the absence of charge-discharge processes. This relatively dispersed action may be the key factor that renders the recovery capacity less "stable." Therefore, the degrading capacity in Model 1 can be referred to as "underlying capacity," the increased capacity in Model 2 can be referred to as "recovery capacity," and the degrading capacity in Model 3 can be referred to as "surface capacity." The measured actual capacity consists of a combination of underlying and surface capacities, where the underlying capacity's degradation is irreversible, while the surface capacity can be replenished multiple times.
A battery aging model has been developed to account for the three types of capacities corresponding to the three models.

If the battery is not rested for a long time during the decay process, it should follow the normal decay rate, and the attenuation curve represents the underlying capacity. When modeling the surface capacity, the influence of the underlying capacity should be removed. During the experimental process, it is inevitable to encounter situations where the experiment needs to be temporarily paused due to some uncontrollable factors. When the pause duration is relatively long, only the capacity recovery phenomenon caused by the suspension is usually taken into account. This paper adopts a crosscutting translation method to obtain the change process. Due to only considering the recovery phenomenon of the battery after a long resting time, the final method adopted is to horizontally cut out multiple segments of data with obvious resting time and recovery capacity before merging the data. Fig. 3 shows the comparison between the actual capacity and the underlying capacity of the six data groups.

From the figures above, it can be observed that the underlying capacity decreases at a faster rate compared to the original capacity. This can be attributed to the significant capacity recovery that occurs after the battery has been rested for a long time, resulting in a slower overall decline rate. Furthermore, when the capacity of the battery recovers, the recovery capacity tends to decay at a faster rate. The overall variation trend of the underlying capacity can be fitted with a linear curve, and the fitting formula is shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
C = at + b

In Eq.(1), the parameters a and b represent the slope and intercept of the function, respectively. These values can be determined by fitting the mathematical relationship between the experimental time and the underlying capacity, as illustrated in Fig. 4.

From the figures presented above, it can be observed that the variation trend of the underlying capacity can be accurately captured by utilizing a linear curve. Additionally, it is worth noting that as the temperature decreases, the distribution of points becomes more scattered. The results of the fitting parameters are summarized in Table 2.

To further assess the accuracy of the fitting, Pearson's coefficient (Pearson's r), degrees of freedom (DF), the ratio of regression sum of squares to total deviation sum of squares (R2), and residual sum of squares (SSE) were selected as evaluation criteria. The corresponding results are presented in Table 3.

In Table 3, "Count" refers to the number of data points, and the degree of freedom represents the number of data points with unrestricted values. The results show that the degree of freedom for each dataset is close to the total number of points. Pearson's coefficient measures the degree of linear correlation between two variables, with values closer to −1 indicating a stronger negative linear correlation. In this case, Pearson's coefficient for each dataset is lower than −0.95, indicating a confidence level exceeding 95 %. Additionally, all datasets exhibit an R2 value exceeding 0.9, which demonstrates a high level of goodness of fit for the function.

In Section 2.2, it is mentioned that capacity recovery takes place when the battery is rested for a long period [50]. When the resting time is 0, the capacity should follow the underlying attenuation rate, and there is no capacity recovery at this point. Therefore, the recovery capacity model can be represented as Eq. (2).

In Eq. (3), the parameter c represents a constant. The variable t represents the resting time, which can be categorized into two types: the resting time after the last discharge and the resting time after the current charge. Due to the short duration of the latter in the paper's experiments, both types of resting time are considered to have the same effect on recovery capacity and can be added directly. The results demonstrate that the exponential model effectively predicts the surface capacity of each battery. To further assess the accuracy of the model, several indices were selected, including the root-mean-square error (RMSE) and R2. The corresponding results are presented in Table 4.

In the table provided, R2 represents the ratio of the sum of squares of regression to the sum of squares of total deviations. A value closer to 1 indicates higher accuracy. RMSE is the square root of the ratio between the deviation between observed and true values and the number of observations. The R2 values for all six datasets exceed 95 %, indicating a high level of accuracy. Additionally, the RMSE decreases as the experimental temperature decreases, suggesting that the recovery capacity data of battery capacity at low temperatures align more closely with the model proposed in this paper.

The surface capacity represents the cumulative process of capacity recovery, obtained by separating it from the underlying capacity. As mentioned in Section 2.2, the decay rate of surface capacity is faster than that of underlying capacity. If the surface capacity is not fully attenuated and the capacity recovery phenomenon occurs again, the accumulation effect occurs. Since the surface capacity still undergoes capacity attenuation in nature, a proportional model is employed to simulate this process, as illustrated in Eq. (4).

Based on the information provided in Table 5, it can be observed that the change rate of the surface capacity is significantly higher than that of the underlying capacity. This can be attributed to the fact that the surface capacity only recovers a portion of the lithium-ion concentration near the positive and negative electrodes. Once the charging and discharging cycle begins again, the recovered lithium-ion will be consumed at a faster rate, leading to a considerably accelerated decay rate. In terms of the fitting effect, it is evident that the accuracy of the model improves with decreasing temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RUL prediction based on the CPCSPF algorithm
Improvement of the cuckoo search algorithm
ce:section.ce:para:
A type of optimization algorithm, known as the cuckoo search algorithm, was inspired by the brood parasitic behavior of cuckoos. The algorithm operates on the principle that cuckoos search for the most suitable nests and migrate their eggs to the nests of other birds [51]. The following assumptions are made in this algorithm:
(a)
Cuckoos randomly place an egg in the nests of other birds while removing one egg from its original nest to maintain the same number of eggs.
(b)
There is a certain probability P
a that the mother bird will detect and reject the cuckoo egg. If this occurs, the nest will be abandoned, and the detected egg will not survive.
(c)
Only the most well-adapted cuckoo eggs have a higher chance survive in the host nests.

In the context of the algorithm, the term "nest" refers to a solution. The process of a cuckoo randomly searching for a nest corresponds to the search for an optimal solution. The possibility of a cuckoo egg being found represents the probability of the solution being abandoned. The quality of a nest is determined by whether the cuckoo egg hatches successfully or not.

The cuckoo search algorithm has several advantages, including fewer parameters, lower computational complexity, and strong global optimization capabilities. However, it is known to have a weakness in local optimization, which can lead to a decline in population diversity in later cycles. In this paper, the Cauchy perturbation factor is introduced to address this issue and improve the algorithm's ability to continue optimizing and avoid getting trapped in local optima. This factor essentially facilitates a secondary local optimization process. The flowchart illustrating the steps of the algorithm is presented in Fig. 6.

Step 2. Determining the current Time-Optimal solution. The fitness of each solution in the population is evaluated to determine the optimal solution at the current time. The fitness calculation is performed to assess how well each solution satisfies the objectives or constraints of the problem. The solution with the highest fitness value is considered the current time-optimal solution.

Step 3. Checking for local optimum. In this step, the algorithm checks if a local optimum has been reached. This is determined by evaluating whether the fitness value meets a certain condition, as shown in Eq. (6). If the condition is met, it indicates that a local optimum has been reached.

Step 6. Determining the current time-optimal solution. The fitness of each solution is calculated to identify the solution with the highest fitness as the optimal solution at the current time.

Step 7. Random migration.The smaller the value of P

Step 9. Determination of iteration stops. Check if the current iteration number has reached the maximum value. If it has, terminate the loop. Otherwise, return to Step 2 and continue the iteration.

By introducing the Cauchy perturbation factor, the cuckoo search algorithm's local optimization ability is effectively improved. Furthermore, to accurately predict the RUL and identify the three model parameters in this paper, it is necessary to combine the improved cuckoo search algorithm with the particle filtering algorithm. This integrated approach will facilitate the development of a new algorithm capable of achieving accurate RUL prediction.

Based on the capacity attenuation model established in Section 2, the parameters to be identified include a, c, d, then the state vector X is defined as [a, c, d] T , and the measurement signal Z is the estimation value of capacity. When the capacity degradation model created in Section 2.5 is used to forecast the battery capacity decline process, data noise will unavoidably occur due to the discrepancy between the measured data and the real data, which causes the errors to continuously accumulate and cause instability in the RUL prediction. The rationality and correctness of the noise processing will determine how accurate the algorithm is. Therefore, the state transfer equation and the measurement equation are shown in Eq. (10).

Based on the standard PF algorithm, the CPCSPF algorithm for the RUL prediction of the LIB is proposed by introducing the ICS algorithm in the stage of importance sampling. The framework of the overall construction of RUL prediction and the flowchart of the CPCSPF algorithm is shown in Fig. 7. The steps of the algorithm are as follows.

Step 3.8. Check to see if the maximum number of iterations has been achieved. End the cycle and output the new particles set if the current iterative number reaches the maximum iterative number (in this work, T is 500 times); otherwise, go back to Step 3.2 and repeat the loop.

In practical application, a certain amount of battery capacity attenuation data should be imported to train model parameters. The probability density function is obtained when the estimated capacity reaches the threshold of 20 % attenuation.

To evaluate the robustness and superiority of the proposed CPCSPF algorithm, experiments were conducted using four different cycle sets: 100 cycles, 150 cycles, 250 cycles, and 350 cycles. These cycle sets were chosen to verify the algorithm's performance under various conditions. In addition, to demonstrate the superiority of the CPCSPF algorithm for RUL prediction, two commonly used algorithms, namely PF (Particle Filtering) and UPF (Unscented Particle Filtering), were selected for comparison. The prediction results of RUL obtained from these algorithms were compared with those of the CPCSPF algorithm. By comparing the performance of the CPCSPF algorithm with PF and UPF algorithms, the study aims to highlight the advantages of the proposed algorithm in terms of RUL prediction accuracy and effectiveness.

Fig. 8(a)-(d) presents the capacity prediction results for A1∼A4 datasets. In each subfigure, the magenta dotted line represents the failure threshold, which is set at 80 % (0.88Ah) of the rated capacity (1.1Ah) based on previous research [45]. The solid green line perpendicular to the horizontal axis represents the cutoff time of the introduced cycles. The solid blue line represents the capacity prediction results obtained using the PF algorithm, while the solid magenta line represents the results obtained using the UPF algorithm. The solid orange line represents the predicted results obtained using the proposed CPCSPF algorithm.

Observing the plots, it can be seen that as more data is imported, the overall capacity prediction results become increasingly closer to the actual attenuation data. Additionally, the accuracy advantage of the algorithm proposed in this paper over the PF and UPF algorithms becomes more pronounced. This indicates that the proposed algorithm has the potential to be applied for accurate estimation of battery RUL. Furthermore, this paper extends the RUL estimation to the A5 and A6 datasets, providing additional evidence of the algorithm's applicability and effectiveness.

Fig. 9(a)-(b) illustrates the capacity and probability density function (PDF) of the RUL prediction results for two datasets, A5 and A6, at −15 ℃, respectively. In each subfigure, the blue-filled area represents the PDF prediction area obtained using the PF algorithm. The magenta-filled area represents the PDF prediction area obtained using the UPF algorithm. The orange-filled area represents the PDF prediction area obtained using the CPCSPF algorithm.
It is important to note that the PDF values have been multiplied by 0.2 for visualization, and this scaling factor does not affect the actual prediction results of RUL.
The combined analysis of the capacity prediction and PDF prediction areas provides a comprehensive understanding of the RUL estimation results for the A5 and A6 datasets at −15 ℃.

The temperature mentioned (−15 ℃) represents the lowest temperature during winter in the North China Plain. It is known that such extreme temperatures can have a significant impact on the performance of various batteries, including columnar lithium-ion batteries.

However, the algorithm proposed in this paper demonstrates a strong ability to adapt and perform well even under such low-temperature conditions. It shows a good following effect, indicating its effectiveness in accurately estimating the remaining useful life (RUL) of batteries, even when they are subjected to challenging environmental conditions.

This is an important finding as it highlights the robustness and reliability of the algorithm, making it suitable for practical applications where batteries may be exposed to extreme temperature variations.

To evaluate the performance of the proposed CPCSPF algorithm, a comparison was made with the PF and UPF algorithms using the same dataset for RUL estimation. Several metrics were selected to assess the accuracy and effectiveness of the algorithms, including:

Absolute Error (AE): It calculates the absolute difference between the predicted RUL values and the actual RUL values. Smaller AE values indicate better precision. Relative Error (RE): It represents the percentage difference between the predicted RUL values and the actual RUL values. Lower RE values indicate better performance.PDF Width: It measures the width of the Probability Density Function (PDF) for RUL prediction. A narrower PDF indicates a more precise estimation. Resampling Rate: It indicates the rate at which resampling is performed in the particle filtering algorithm. A lower resampling rate suggests a more efficient and accurate estimation.

By comparing these metrics among the CPCSPF, PF, and UPF algorithms, the study aims to assess the superiority of the proposed algorithm in terms of accuracy, precision, estimation width, and computational efficiency for RUL prediction.

Fig. 10 shows the RMSE of capacity decay trend prediction under two datasets A5 and A6. The results show that RMSE decreases with the increase of the imported capacity data, and in the same cycle, the prediction results of the proposed algorithm show a smaller degree of dispersion, which is of positive significance to the prediction accuracy of RUL.

Table 6 shows the comparison of RUL prediction results of the A6 data set. The overall results show that under all inspection cycles, the CPCSPF algorithm can provide the highest accuracy prediction results of RUL, with a minimum relative error of only 0.84 %. While the prediction results of the corresponding PF and UPF algorithms are both more than 1.5 %. The main reason is that the CPCSPF algorithm improves the global optimization ability of PF and reduces the resampling rate. The resampling rate is the ratio of the number of resampling particles to the total number of particles. The lower the resampling rate is, the weaker the particle attenuation phenomenon is, the lower the computation complexity is, and the more practical the algorithm is. As shown in the above table, the average resampling of the CPCSPF algorithm is less than 50 % and decreases with the increase of the number of cycles. Meanwhile, PF and UPF algorithms are both over 60 %. Therefore, the proposed algorithm can contain the particle decay phenomenon more effectively. PDF width refers to the width of the posterior probability density function predicted by RUL, and the narrower it is, the higher the prediction confidence is. Compared with PF and UPF algorithms, the PDF width of the proposed algorithm in this paper has obvious advantages and higher reliability. In summary, compared with PF and UPF algorithms, the CPCSPF algorithm proposed is undoubtedly a better choice for RUL prediction.

In addition, compared with the latest model-driven RUL research, including the improved cuckoo search particle filter (ICS-PF) algorithm model proposed by Xianghui Qiu et al. [52], the chaotic particle swarm optimization-particle filtering (CPSO-PF) algorithm model proposed by Li-Hua Ye et al. [53], and the innovative technique of F-distribution PF and Kernel Smoothing (FPFKS) algorithm model proposed by Kai Zhang et al. [54], the maximum relative error, maximum PDF width, and maximum resampling rate are shown in Table 7.

The results indicate that the CP-CS-PF algorithm model proposed in this paper exhibits significant performance advantages compared to the other three algorithms. Specifically, the Max RE is 8.16 % lower than CPSO-PF and 10.16 % lower than FPFKS, while it is 48.76 % higher than ICS-PF. Additionally, the Max PDF width is 45.83 % lower than ICS-PF and 74.17 % lower than CPSO-PF, with a 74.35 % increase compared to FPFKS. Furthermore, the Max Resampling rate is 1.18 % lower than ICS-PF. These comparative results demonstrate that the proposed model in this paper excels in terms of accuracy, convergence, and particle diversity.

In this paper, a kind of Cauchy perturbation cuckoo search particle filtering algorithm is presented to achieve an accurate prediction of the RUL of lithium-ion batteries. The main idea is to improve the local optimization ability of the cuckoo search algorithm by introducing the Cauchy perturbation factor and then ameliorating the global optimization accuracy of the particle filtering algorithm. Then, the battery RUL is obtained by acquiring the posterior probability density. The model established in this paper takes into account the capacity recovery phenomenon caused by the long-term resting of batteries. The variation law of battery capacity is fully considered by separating surface capacity, underlying capacity, and recovery capacity and modeling separately. Finally, six battery datasets were chosen to test the effectiveness of the suggested methodology. It offers a fresh viewpoint on the battery RUL prognostic technique.

However, it is important to acknowledge that the proposed method has a disadvantage in terms of its relatively complex modeling process, which requires numerous operations and function-fitting steps. Additionally, the performance of the proposed method is highly dependent on the accuracy of the model used. In light of this, future research will be conducted to address and improve upon these limitations. The goal is to develop a more streamlined and efficient modeling process, reducing complexity and enhancing the robustness of the method.
